title: UNRIO: Uncertainty-Aware Velocity Learning for Radar-Inertial Odometry
abstract: 我们提出了UNRIO，一种基于不确定性感知的雷达-惯性里程计系统，它直接从原始毫米波雷达IQ信号而非经过处理的点云数据中估计自身速度。现有雷达-惯性里程计方法依赖人工设计的信号处理流程，这些流程会丢弃原始频谱中的潜在信息，且需要精细的参数调试。为解决这一问题，我们基于GRT架构提出了一种Transformer神经网络，通过处理完整的4维频谱立方体，以两种模式预测本体坐标系速度：直接线性速度估计和逐角度多普勒速度图。该网络采用三阶段训练策略：基于激光雷达投影深度的几何预训练、速度/多普勒精调、以及通过负对数似然损失进行的不确定性校准，使其能够在输出预测的同时生成不确定性估计。这些不确定性估计被传播至滑动窗口位姿图中，该图融合了雷达速度因子与IMU预积分测量。我们在IQ1M数据集上对UNRIO进行训练和评估，涵盖多种室内环境的正向运动及未见过的侧向运动模式。该方法在大多数序列中实现了最低的相对位姿误差，尤其在侧向运动轨迹上——当稀疏点云导致传统速度估计器性能下降时，我们的方法相比经典DSP基线取得了显著优势。
html_content:
UNRIO: Uncertainty-Aware Velocity Learning
for Radar-Inertial Odometry
Jui-Te Huang, Tinashu Huang, Anthony Rowe
and Michael Kaess
J. Huang, T. Huang, A, Rowe and M. Kaess are, with the Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA 15213, USA
{juiteh, tianshu2, agr, kaess}@andrew.cmu.edu
Abstract
We present UNRIO, an uncertainty-aware radar-inertial odometry system that estimates ego-velocity directly from raw mmWave radar IQ signals rather than processed point clouds. Existing radar-inertial odometry methods rely on handcrafted signal processing pipelines that discard latent information in the raw spectrum and require careful parameter tuning. To address this, we propose a transformer-based neural network built on the GRT architecture that processes the full 4-D spectral cube to predict body-frame velocity in two modes: a direct linear velocity estimate and a per-angle-bin Doppler velocity map. The network is trained in three stages — geometric pretraining on LiDAR-projected depth, velocity or Doppler fine-tuning, and uncertainty calibration via negative log-likelihood loss — enabling it to produce uncertainty estimates alongside its predictions. These uncertainty estimates are propagated into a sliding-window pose graph that fuses radar velocity factors with IMU preintegration measurements. We train and evaluate UNRIO on the IQ1M dataset across diverse indoor environments with both forward and lateral motion patterns unseen during training. Our method achieves the lowest relative pose error on the majority of sequences, with particularly strong gains over classical DSP baselines on lateral-motion trajectories where sparse point clouds degrade conventional velocity estimators.
I
Introduction
mmWave radar sensing technology has enabled simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) in low-visibility, low-lighting, and adverse environmental conditions where LiDAR and RGB cameras fail to provide reliable sensing. Recent advances in radar state estimation
[
4
,
27
,
29
,
11
,
16
,
20
]
have demonstrated the potential of radar sensors for localization. However, all of the above works rely on signal-processed radar point clouds, while the use of raw complex radar signals for state estimation remains largely unexplored.
Modern commercially available mmWave radars offer many design choices
[
14
]
. Manufacturers support a wide range of signal configurations, antenna designs, and layouts, paired with proprietary signal processing pipelines that typically produce point clouds with per-point Doppler velocity for downstream tasks. The most common approach employs frequency-modulated continuous wave (FMCW) waveforms with a multi-input multi-output (MIMO) antenna array. The signal processing algorithm usually starts with multi-dimensional Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detection, and angle-of-arrival estimation. However, this pipeline discards a significant amount of information latent in the raw signal, while also introducing numerous heuristic parameters that require careful tuning and whose performance varies with the material properties and reflectivity of the environment. Only recently has work
[
13
]
begun to explore the potential of training a foundation model on large-scale datasets to replace such handcrafted signal processing pipelines.
Figure 1:
Our radar-inertial odometry system using raw mmWave radar spectrum as input.
Our system predicts velocity and its uncertainty from the radar spectrum using a Transformer neural network trained on a large dataset with diverse motion patterns.
The idea of leveraging machine learning with large datasets for front-end processing in SLAM has been explored extensively in visual odometry
[
23
,
25
,
24
]
, where learned features and confidence weights are incorporated into the optimization stage to improve robustness and accuracy. A similar direction was explored by Lu et al.
[
19
]
, who trained a model to directly regress odometry from radar and IMU inputs. However, their approach relied on the lossy processed point cloud representation and struggled to generalize across environments.
In this work, we exploit the full information contained in the raw radar signal to build a more robust radar-inertial state estimation system that generalizes to novel environments and improves on different motion patterns. Our main contributions are as follows:
•
We present a learning-based method that estimates ego-velocity directly from raw radar spectrum data, supporting two output modes: a single linear velocity estimate and a per-angle-bin Doppler velocity map.
•
We extend the model to predict uncertainty alongside the velocity estimate, which is subsequently propagated into our radar-inertial odometry framework.
•
We train the our model on a large-scale raw radar spectrum dataset and introduce UNRIO, a radar-inertial odometry algorithm. We evaluate its state estimation performance on held-out environments with diverse forward and lateral motion patterns.
II
Related Work
II-A
Radar-Inertial Odometry
Since the early developments of the field, research on mmWave radar for state estimation has been using radar-generated point clouds with Doppler velocity. Works from Doer et al.
[
4
,
5
]
build a baseline radar-inertial odometry system estimating body-frame velocity using RANSAC and fusing it with IMU via EKF filtering. Building on their work, Kim et al.
[
16
]
further extend the EKF with online temporal calibration. Huang et al.
[
11
]
use a fixed-lag window optimization strategy to fuse multiple radar-estimated velocities with IMU in an optimization window.
With more antennas and higher angular resolution on recent commercial mmWave radars, other works incorporate scan-matching strategies from LiDAR state estimation systems. Zhuang et al. integrated body velocity, IMU, and scan-to-map matching in their radar SLAM system: 4D iRIOM
[
29
]
. Herraez et al.
[
10
]
build a full SLAM system with radar and IMU where the main odometry sources come from radar point scan-to-map matching.
Despite all these developments, no works have attempted to leverage full radar spectrum information for state estimation, with the closest being
[
15
]
which discusses the effect of signal processing on a radar odometry system while still relying on generated point clouds.
II-B
Machine Learning for Radar Spectrum
While most radar odometry systems — and radar perception systems more broadly — rely on point clouds, point cloud extraction discards much of the information contained in a raw 4D radar cube. Competing approaches instead propose to directly interpret the cube by training neural networks with various architectures on large volumes of data, and are able to achieve superior resolution and accuracy on tasks ranging from object detection
[
26
,
8
,
2
]
and super-resolution
[
22
,
13
]
to camera-like semantic segmentation
[
17
,
13
]
and multi-view 3D reconstruction
[
12
,
1
,
18
]
.
In particular, models trained using foundation model-style pretraining have been shown to be highly scalable with increased training data
[
13
]
. Increasingly capable edge compute systems have also made deploying ML-based radar perception to real time systems
[
6
]
much more practical. Together, this raises the possibility of using large-scale datasets to pretrain powerful, general-purpose models for radar perception, which are then distilled and deployed to autonomous robots and vehicles.
II-C
Uncertainty Prediction for State Estimation
While learning-based methods are being adopted by the SLAM research community, several works have explored learned uncertainty estimation. Teed et al.
[
24
]
propose DROID-SLAM, which utilizes a differentiable bundle adjustment layer to implicitly tune the uncertainty model. Dexheimer et al.
[
3
]
proposed a learned depth covariance function applied in downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction. To improve the reliability of the feature tracking process, some methods introduce learning-based uncertainty measurements or confidence scores, such as Wang et al.
[
25
]
and Murai et al.
[
21
]
, which leverage learned 3D reconstruction priors for dense SLAM. Similarly, Qiu et al.
[
23
]
propose MAC-VO, a learning-based stereo visual odometry framework that trains a metrics-aware covariance model to select keypoints and weight residuals in pose graph optimization.
Similar ideas have been explored in LiDAR-based state estimation, where Zhao et al.
[
28
]
propose SuperLoc, a robust LiDAR-inertial localization system that predicts alignment risks from raw sensor measurements before optimization. Inspired by these works, we apply the same idea of learned velocity and uncertainty estimation to radar spectrum data for state estimation.
III
Methodology
In this section, we present our learning-based method for processing raw IQ mmWave radar signals to estimate body-frame velocity and its uncertainty. The predicted velocity and uncertainty are then fused with IMU measurements in a sliding-window pose graph optimization to recover the odometry trajectory.
III-A
Signal Processing
In this work, we use a typical commercially available mmWave radar that transmit frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) which emits a sequence of chirps across an antenna array in an multi-input multi-output (MIMO) mode. The raw output of a single frame is a complex IQ tensor of shape
(

III-B
Neural Network Architecture
We build our velocity and uncertainty prediction network based on the GRT
[
13
]
transformer architecture. We experiment with two types of velocity prediction: one that directly outputs the linear body-frame velocity, and another that outputs per-angle-bin Doppler values. To enable uncertainty estimation, we design the network to have separate decoder heads for prediction and uncertainty outputs. All network components are listed as follows:

The spectral cube

The elevation and azimuth axes are first collapsed into the channel dimension via a learned linear layer, after which spatial patches are applied over the Doppler and range axes, respectively. Each patch is projected to a

III-C
Training Strategy
In order to make the model generalize, our model training proceeds in three stages, each building on the trained model weights from the previous stage.
Stage 1 — Depth pretraining.

Figure 2:
Qualitative trajectory comparison on six representative sequences.
Ground truth is shown as a dashed black line; estimated trajectories from PC, GRT, Doppler, and Velocity are shown as solid colored lines, aligned to the ground truth via SE(3) Umeyama alignment. Insets highlight regions of interest where method differences are most pronounced. Lateral-motion sequences (all except
posner.2.fwd
) demonstrate the clear advantage of learning-based methods over the classical DSP point-cloud baseline.
IV
Experiments
IV-A
Radar IQ Dataset and Training
We train and evaluate our proposed methods with the IQ1M dataset
[
13
]
, which contains about 29 hours, 1 million frames at 20 frames per second of complex signal data collected from a Texas Instruments AWR1843Boost radar and a DCA-1000EVM capture card. The IMU measurements are provided by a XSens MTi-3 AHRS sensor running at 100 frames per second. The ground truth depth and velocity are provided by the dataset which were built using the LiDAR state estimation algorithm.
The IQ1M dataset has three types of radar chirp configurations collected in three different scenes and motion patterns (indoor, outdoor, bike). We use the full training set for depth pretraining. For the second-stage velocity and third-stage uncertainty training, we only train on the indoor training subset, which has a chirp configuration enabling a maximum Doppler velocity of 1.2 m/s and a maximum range of 11.2 m. For the evaluation stage, we run and evaluate our odometry algorithm on the indoor testing set, which comprises three indoor environments not seen during training.
Due to the limited antenna radiation pattern of the AWR1843Boost radar, which focuses primarily on the azimuth plane and introduces noise along the elevation axis, we restrict pose estimation and evaluation to the 2D plane by fixing the marginalized pose to
z
=
0
z=0
.
IV-B
Baseline
We compare our proposed method With two baseline methods to process radar spectrum into velocity. We evaluate the generated velocity with same radar-inertial odometry system.
IV-B
1
CFAR generate point cloud
We follow the classical radar digital signal processing pipeline from
Texas Instruments mmWave Studio
[
14
]
to convert raw IQ samples into a 3-D point cloud through three sequential stages: spectral processing, detection, and angle-of-arrival estimation. We first perform a 4-D FFT as described in Section
III-A
. One small difference is that we apply a DC-removal step by subtracting the mean along the slow-time dimension before the FFT, which removes the zero-Doppler clutter. We then apply 2-D cell-averaging CFAR to detect candidate objects in the Range-Doppler map. For each detection, we select the peak-magnitude bin in its corresponding elevation-azimuth FFT image. Each bin

IV-B

We used the pretrained model from GRT
[

with velocity prediction task trained to generate the velocity. Where in GRT the velocity output scale is normalized according to Doppler resolution. We convert it back to metric scale by multiply it with Doppler resolution according to the indoor data chirp configuration. The covariance of the velocity are fixed as

IV-C
Quantitative Evaluation
We evaluate our radar-inertial odometry using the EVO
[
9
]
library. Tables
II
and
II
report the Absolute Pose Error (APE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE) of the translation component across all testing sequences, separated by dominant motion type: forward and lateral. APE is computed with Umeyama alignment, and RPE is evaluated at 10-meter intervals.
Our method achieves the lowest RPE in the majority of sequences, demonstrating superior local accuracy compared to all baselines. The advantage is particularly evident in the lateral-motion sequences (Table
II
), where learning-based estimators consistently outperform the classical DSP baseline by a margin; with lateral motion, DSP produces fewer points along the direction of motion, whereas our approach leverages the full Doppler spectrum to maintain accuracy even when the point cloud is sparse or poorly conditioned. In forward-motion sequences (Table
II
), the gap narrows since radial velocity is better observed, yet our method still achieves competitive APE and the best RPE in most traces.
IV-D
Qualitative Evaluation
Figure
2
shows representative trajectory
comparisons across lateral and forward-motion sequences. In the lateral sequences, the DSP point-cloud baseline (PC) exhibits large drift, often deviating significantly from the ground truth. The GRT baseline shows inconsistent behavior, competitive on some traces yet failing on others. In contrast, the Doppler and Velocity estimators track the ground truth closely throughout, with the zoomed insets revealing that the Velocity method maintains tighter agreement in regions where the other methods diverge. For the forward-motion sequence (
posner.2.fwd
), all methods improve, consistent with the quantitative results in Table
II
, as radial Doppler geometry is better conditioned in the direction of travel. Nevertheless, the most notable advantage of our learning-based estimators is in lateral-motion sequences, where they consistently maintain trajectory smoothness and accuracy while classical DSP-based methods exhibit significant drift.
V
Conclusion
We presented UNRIO, a radar-inertial odometry system that estimates ego-velocity and its uncertainty directly from raw mmWave radar IQ signals, bypassing the information loss inherent in classical signal processing pipelines. Our transformer-based network supports both direct velocity and per-angle-bin Doppler output modes, each with calibrated uncertainty estimates that are propagated into a sliding-window pose graph fusing radar and IMU measurements.
Evaluations on held-out indoor environments from the IQ1M dataset show that UNRIO achieves state-of-the-art relative pose error across most sequences, with the largest gains on lateral-motion trajectories where classical DSP-based point cloud methods struggle due to sparse radial observations. These results demonstrate the value of operating on the full radar spectrum for robust, generalizable state estimation. Future work will focus on extending to different radar modulations and configurations, incorporating mapping capabilities, and improving cross-environment generalization.

preview_text:
We present UNRIO, an uncertainty-aware radar-inertial odometry system that estimates ego-velocity directly from raw mmWave radar IQ signals rather than processed point clouds. Existing radar-inertial odometry methods rely on handcrafted signal processing pipelines that discard latent information in the raw spectrum and require careful parameter tuning. To address this, we propose a transformer-based neural network built on the GRT architecture that processes the full 4-D spectral cube to predict body-frame velocity in two modes: a direct linear velocity estimate and a per-anglebin Doppler velocity map. The network is trained in three stages: geometric pretraining on LiDAR-projected depth, velocity or Doppler fine-tuning, and uncertainty calibration via negative log-likelihood loss, enabling it to produce uncertainty estimates alongside its predictions. These uncertainty estimates are propagated into a sliding-window pose graph that fuses radar velocity factors with IMU preintegration measurements. We train and evaluate UNRIO on the IQ1M dataset across diverse indoor environments with both forward and lateral motion patterns unseen during training. Our method achieves the lowest relative pose error on the majority of sequences, with particularly strong gains over classical DSP baselines on Lateral-motion trajectories where sparse point clouds degrade conventional velocity estimators.

UNRIO: Uncertainty-Aware Velocity Learning
for Radar-Inertial Odometry
Jui-Te Huang, Tinashu Huang, Anthony Rowe
and Michael Kaess
J. Huang, T. Huang, A, Rowe and M. Kaess are, with the Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA 15213, USA
{juiteh, tianshu2, agr, kaess}@andrew.cmu.edu
Abstract
We present UNRIO, an uncertainty-aware radar-inertial odometry system that estimates ego-velocity directly from raw mmWave radar IQ signals rather than processed point clouds. Existing radar-inertial odometry methods rely on handcrafted signal processing pipelines that discard latent information i
